title: Receiving investors in the block market for corporate bonds
abstract: We study block trades in the corporate bond market, where dealers buy or sell blocks from initiating customers and offset their positions with receiving investors. Our findings indicate that while receivers benefit from trading cost savings, they primarily bear adverse selection costs and experience worse outcomes when informed trading is prevalent. Mandatory trade reporting improves receiver outcomes by revealing dealers’ private information, but the benefits are reduced when reporting is delayed. Our results emphasize the importance of transparency regime design and suggest potential market fragility: if information asymmetry becomes severe, receivers may withdraw from the block market.

author.Stacey Jacobsen: Cox School of Business, Southern Methodist University, Dallas TX 75025, United States
author.Kumar Venkataraman: Cox School of Business, Southern Methodist University, Dallas TX 75025, United States

article.1. Introduction: We examine the block market's role in facilitating large trades in corporate bonds. Transactions over $15 million have comprised nearly 15 % of corporate bond volume over the past two decades, highlighting the block market as an important liquidity source for institutional investors. Block trading generally involves a sequence of negotiated, non-anonymous, and asynchronous transactions: an “initiating” block trade between the institution and the dealer, typically facilitated in a principal capacity, and the dealer's related trades with “receiving” investors to offset the block position. Receiving investors play an important role in the theoretical models of the block market, yet they have received little attention in the empirical literature.The goal of this article is to study outcomes for receiving investors (henceforth, receivers) to whom dealers distribute block positions. Theoretical models, such as those by Burdett and O'Hara (1987), Naik et al. (1999) and Back et al. (2020), depict a setting in which a dealer acquires a block position and then disposes of it through a series of transactions. Given that informed traders tend to transact in larger quantities, information asymmetry plays a crucial role in the block market. In these models, the dealer typically possesses less information than the customer initiating the block trade (henceforth, initiator). However, the dealer may partially infer the trade's motive by observing signals such as block size, price, and initiator identity during bilateral negotiations. The least informed is the receiver who is approached by the dealer interested in trading out of the block position. In the framework of Burdett and O'Hara (1987), receivers lack information regarding the yet-to-be-reported block trade, its size and the initiator's identity, making it difficult for them to anticipate the block's information effects fully. In this setting of heightened adverse selection risk, understanding the economics of receiver participation is important for validating the well-developed theoretical models and for designing a well-functioning block market.We focus on the corporate bond block market for several reasons. First, in comparison to equities which trade frequently, corporate bonds are substantially less liquid, subjecting dealers to greater inventory risk on block positions. The Volcker rule has reduced dealers’ willingness to commit capital to market making in corporate bonds,1 thereby increasing the importance of receivers, who supply liquidity to dealers, for maintaining a resilient block market.Second, most publicly available databases, such as Trade and Quote (TAQ) data for U.S. equities, do not identify the parties involved in a trade. As a result, even the most fundamental theoretical predictions about receivers remain untested, as the relationships hypothesized in theory may be obscured in the available transactions data which represent a mixture of trader types. Our study addresses this gap in the literature by utilizing the enhanced corporate bond TRACE transaction data, which captures the entire history of the dealers’ trades with counterparties. We leverage these unique data features to develop a methodology for identifying the initiating block trade and the dealer's related trades with receivers.2 This identification process is more precise in markets where trading is sparse, as is typical with corporate bonds.Third, unlike equities that are traded on highly transparent exchanges, corporate bonds are traded in a relatively opaque over-the-counter (OTC) market, where quotations are available only to select market participants, and completed trades are often subject to reporting delays. In equity markets, an initiator can discreetly execute a large block of shares by breaking it into small trades on organized, anonymous exchanges. However, in the OTC corporate bond market, the non-anonymous bilateral negotiations with dealers make it challenging for the initiator to hide a block trade. These structural differences grant dealers significant market power over their customers and exacerbate the information asymmetry among block participants in the corporate bond market compared to the equity market.In this article, we study a sample of 205,104 corporate bond block transactions, each exceeding $15 million, alongside 690,418 related offsetting trades of dealers with receivers, covering the period from 2002 to 2021. Our results indicate that dealers typically offset a block position with three to four counterparties. The average size of these receiver trades is $9 million, suggesting that receivers are predominantly institutional investors. We examine who benefits and who bears the costs – whether it is the initiator, the dealer, or the receiver – in scenarios where adverse selection risk is high, such as when initiators have private information. Additionally, we study how trade disclosure affects block participants by comparing transparent versus opaque regimes, focusing on the introduction of mandatory post-trade reporting (“transparency” events), and the impact of rules pertaining to reductions in the trade reporting timeframe (“delay” events).Our results highlight the important role of the information environment in maintaining a healthy block market. Receivers typically experience worse outcomes when information effects are pronounced during block trades, whereas dealers can generally anticipate informed trading and manage the adverse selection risk. This raises the question: why do receivers continue to participate in the block market despite bearing the costs of adverse selection? Grossman (1992) posits that receivers are natural counterparties to block initiators with trading desires that are known to the dealer. Our findings support this view and are consistent with Burdett and O'Hara's (1987) prediction that, despite the potential for losses from informed blocks, receivers benefit from efficiently establishing or liquidating positions by participating in offsetting trades with the block dealer. These trading cost savings helps explain the block market's appeal to receivers. However, our results also indicate a potential fragility: if information asymmetry becomes pronounced, it could discourage receivers from participating in block trades.Transparency, or the ability of market participants to observe information about the trading process, is an important determinant of the block market's information environment. Our study aligns with theoretical predictions (e.g., Madhavan, 1995; Back et al., 2020), showing that disclosing block trades can convey private information to the market, leading to more favorable trading terms for receivers from dealers. However, while greater transparency benefits receivers, transparency matters less when trade reporting is delayed. These insights are particularly relevant in the context of recent regulatory proposals concerning transparency in bond markets, which are likely to benefit some participants at the expense of others, as noted by Harris (1992). Much of the regulatory debate on how the timeliness of trade disclosure affects the block market has focused on initiators and dealers. Our study emphasizes the importance of considering the welfare of all participants, especially receivers, who play a vital role in the block market.We begin by characterizing the price effects of block trades, following the existing block literature from equity markets, such as Kraus and Stoll (1972). We estimate both the temporary (liquidity effects) and permanent (information effects) price impact of block trades. Fig. 1 illustrates these price effects in a scenario where the dealer sells a block of bonds to the initiator (Dealer Block Sell).3 In this context, the price of the block trade (PB) is higher than the pre-trade equilibrium value (PPRE), indicating the cost incurred by the block initiator for demanding immediacy from the dealer. The post-trade equilibrium value (PPOST) is higher than pre-trade equilibrium value (PPRE), and the difference reflects the information effects of the block trade initiated by a large buyer.Download: Download high-res image (647KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Block Trading Cost Decomposition for Dealer Block Sell.The figure illustrates the decomposition of block initiator costs in a scenario where a dealer sells a block of bonds to the initiator (Dealer Block Sell) at price PB at time tblock. The block trade price is higher than the pre-trade equilibrium value PPRE at time tPRE. The post-trade equilibrium value (PPOST) at tPOST is higher than the pre-trade equilibrium value. The dealer offsets the block position by purchasing bonds at a weighted-average price POFF from receiving investors at time toffset. In Figure A, block initiator costs of 20 bp are decomposed into a temporary price impact component (15 bp) and a permanent price impact component (5 bp). In Figure B, block initiator costs of 20 bp are further decomposed into dealer spread (10 bp) and receiver spread (5 bp), and a permanent price impact component (5 bp). For a Dealer Block Buy scenario, where the dealer buys a block of bonds from the initiator and sells them to receivers, the decomposition would be analogous except in reverse. Figure C illustrates receiver spreads before and after block report for a block with large information effects. In this example, block initiator costs are 20 bp and permanent price impact is 7.5 bp. Prior to block report, the dealer buys from receiving investors at price POFF-BEF earning a spread of 17.5 bp. Following block report, the dealer buys from receivers at a higher price of POFF-AFT earning a lower spread of 10 bp. Receivers offsetting prior to block report lose 5 bp and receivers offsetting following block report earn a 2.5 bp spread.Adapting the equity market block methodology to the corporate bond market, we decompose the liquidity effects (PB - PPOST) of the block trade into dealer spread and receiver spread. To offset the block sale at price PB, the dealer purchases the bonds at price POFF from receivers, thereby earning the dealer spread (PB - POFF). The primary risks for the dealer lie in finding receivers willing to sell at a sufficiently low price and being able to quickly offset the block position. The receiver spread (POFF - PPOST) compensates receivers for offsetting the dealer's block position. Notably, when information effects are substantial, receivers spreads are negative (i.e., PPOST > POFF for a Dealer Block Sell), indicating that receivers sold the bonds at a lower price than the new equilibrium value established after the block trade. Negative spreads reflect the opportunity cost to receivers for participating in the block market.For our sample of corporate bond block trades, round-trip dealer spreads average 22 basis points (bp). Initiator costs average 18 bp and increase with block size, reflecting that larger blocks often signal higher levels of private information and impose greater inventory risk on dealers. Receiver spreads average −3 bp, with receivers obtaining better terms when dealers are exposed to more significant inventory risk, such as in larger block trades and high yield bonds. The average (median) permanent price impact of these trades is close to zero (1.4 bp). However, the data reveals substantial information effects for some blocks, with the 66th and 75th percentile of permanent price impact at 25 bp and 52 bp, respectively.We explore the effects of asymmetric information on receivers by identifying three settings where the block initiator is more likely to be informed. First, we exploit evidence from prior research that sustained customer buying in the corporate bond market is linked to price discovery while sustained customer selling often leads to price reversals (Cai et al., 2019; Anand et al., 2021). We observe similar buy-sell asymmetry in block trades, where the permanent price impact is higher for Dealer Block Sells than Buys. We find that dealer spreads remain positive and of similar magnitude for Buys and Sells, while receivers fail to negotiate a higher offsetting price (POFF) for Dealer Block Sells, resulting in negative spreads (−12 bp). For Dealer Block Buys, which generally lack information effects, receiver spreads are slightly positive (1 bp). Thus, receivers appear unable to anticipate the information effects of block trades. Second, when sorting blocks into decile portfolios based on ex-post permanent price impact, we observe that receivers fare worse on informed blocks, while dealers’ spreads are relatively unaffected. Third, block trades made prior to major information events such as credit rating changes and large earnings surprises reveal that receivers primarily bear the adverse selection associated with informed blocks.We also examine the economic rationale behind why receivers participate in the block market, testing the Burdett and O'Hara (1987) prediction that receivers gain cost savings in trade execution, particularly for large positions. We estimate these savings using two methods. First, we employ a trading cost model, comparing actual receiver spreads with the model-implied costs they would incur for initiating a hypothetical trade of similar size. This comparison shows that receivers benefit by avoiding the liquidity premium associated with initiating a trade. Second, we compare the offsetting price of receiver trades in the block market with a hypothetical price derived from TRACE trades on the same side of the market as receiver trades. Both methods show comparable savings, and notably, even with information effects, receivers generally fare better (+20 bp for Dealer Block Buys), or at least no worse (+1 bp for Dealer Block Sells) by participating as offsets in block trades rather than trading a similar-sized position independently.We acknowledge the difficulties in estimating price effects and interpreting measures for corporate bonds with infrequent trading. To address these issues, we examine the robustness of our results across sub-samples that account for trade frequency, trade size and the timeliness of TRACE transactions when estimating benchmark prices. We also estimate the trading cost savings for receivers in block trades versus comparable non-block trades using two approaches that consider trade size, market conditions, and the relevant market side. To address the challenges in reliably identifying the initiating block trade, we also conduct robustness analyses on sub-samples where the initiating block is likely classified with more accuracy.We further study how regulations affecting the trading environment impact the block market. Our analysis begins with transparency events, specifically the introduction of mandatory trade reporting for public corporate bonds in 2003, 2004 and 2005, as well as for non-public 144A corporate bonds in June 2014. Consistent with the empirical literature, we find that the introduction of trade reporting reduces dealer spreads in the block market.4 However, the impact of trade reporting on customers is nuanced and varies with customer type. As theories such as Madhavan (1995) suggest, increased transparency benefits less-informed receivers in their negotiations with dealers, while outcomes for better-informed initiators remain largely unaffected.Additionally, we examine delay events, focusing on FINRA's phased reduction in the maximum allowable reporting delay for corporate bonds, which decreased from 75 min to 45 min, then to 30 min, and finally to 15 min, between 2003 and 2006. The permissible delay in reporting trades to the market lies at the heart of many policy debates about bond market structure. Dealers and industry groups argue that real-time block trade reporting makes it difficult for the dealer to gradually unwind a block position. They assert that allowing a reporting delay is crucial, as it gives dealers the opportunity to partially offset the block position before it is disclosed to the market. Recently, regulators have considered various proposals, such as extending the reporting delay for block trades from the current 15 min to 48 h,5 or reducing it to no more than one minute.6 The Back et al. (2020) model predicts that receivers obtain more favorable terms on offsetting trades made after the block trade disclosure compared to those made before it.We provide supporting evidence through a “within block” analysis, controlling for trade, bond issue, and market conditions that may impact trading costs. Within each delay regime, receivers achieve substantial cost reductions (ranging from 5 bp to 11 bp) on offsetting trades that occur after the block trade report compared to those before the report. Dealer spreads exhibit a similar pattern but with opposite signs. Our results highlight the importance of the disclosure regime's design. Specifically, mandatory reporting of block trades benefits receivers but the benefits are diminished when disclosure is delayed.
article.2. Related literature on block trading a: The block market is characterized by heightened adverse selection and inventory risk. Large trades often signal that the initiator is motivated by private information (Easley and O'Hara, 1987). Dealers must also consider the ease of locating receivers and the prices at which they can distribute the block. As per theory, block dealers can reduce the initiator's trading costs by mitigating adverse selection through screening of informed initiators (Seppi, 1990), minimizing search costs by identifying receivers with latent demand (Grossman, 1992), and facilitating risk sharing (Keim and Madhavan, 1996). Empirical studies from the equity market suggest that block trades are typically less information-driven (Madhavan and Cheng, 1997; Booth et al., 2001) and are associated with smaller temporary price effects, which can be attributed to risk sharing mechanisms (Keim and Madhavan, 1996) and trading with a broader base of counterparties (Bessembinder and Venkataraman, 2004).2.1. The literature on large trades in the corporate bond marketPrior studies on corporate bonds find that institutions often trade with a handful of relationship dealers and obtain lower trading costs on these trades (Di Maggio et al., 2017; O'Hara et al., 2018; Hendershott et al., 2020; Nikolova and Wang, 2020; Goldstein et al., 2021; Jurkatis et al., 2024). O'Hara et al. (2018) show that dealers exercise market power, offering better trade terms to more active institutions compared to less active ones. Unlike equities, where trading costs typically increase with trade size, larger corporate bond trades incur lower costs than smaller trades (see Edwards et al., 2007; Goldstein et al., 2007), reducing the incentive to split a block position. As a result, order splitting appears to be less common in corporate bonds than in equities, though Czech and Pintér (2022) document evidence of order splitting across multiple dealers in the UK market.Other studies highlight the significant trading challenges in the bond market, including high search costs, frequent and costly trade failures, and execution delays (Hendershott et al., 2022; Kargar et al., 2022). Goldstein and Hotchkiss (2020) find that dealers’ holding periods decrease with higher credit risk and bond illiquidity, highlighting the role of inventory and search costs. Research on asset-backed securities and municipal bonds by Hollifield et al. (2017) and Li and Schurhoff (2019), respectively, shows the importance of a dealer's centrality in determining trade terms for customers. These findings suggest that receivers may benefit from trading with their relationship dealers with a recently intermediated block position.Our study complements Li et al. (2023), who examine portfolio trading, where dealers bid on a basket of bonds for simultaneous execution. Although the average portfolio trade size is substantial at $61 million, the typical portfolio includes over 100 bond issues, resulting in an individual bond trade size of about $540,000. While Li et al. (2023) focus on block trades that facilitate large-scale portfolio adjustments, our study focuses on block trades that facilitate entry or exit in a single bond. Additionally, our work is related to Choi et al. (2024), who consider customer types in scenarios where trades are quickly paired with an opposite signed trade by the same dealer, effectively acting as a broker. In comparison, we investigate scenarios where dealers participate in a principal capacity, exposing them to inventory and adverse selection risks. We then examine how receiver outcomes are influenced by information asymmetry, trade disclosure rules, and block attributes. We add to the related literature (Anand et al., 2021; Choi et al., 2024) showing that liquidity-supplying customers obtain better outcomes than liquidity-demanding customers.Madhavan (2000) argues that the block market literature has primarily focused on the initiator's perspective, but the benefits may largely accrue to receivers. While research on trading costs of large transactions exist, testing theoretical predictions for specific customer-types has been challenging. We present a method to identify related trades in the block market and analyze outcomes for specific participants, showing that receivers can reduce the explicit costs of building or liquidating positions in the block market, as predicted by Burdett and O'Hara (1987). Our findings demonstrate the block market's appeal to receivers and highlight the importance of the information environment for its effective functioning.2.2. Transparency and the block market for corporate bondsOpaque markets offer advantages to dealers during negotiations with less informed customers (Pagano and Roell, 1996; Green et al., 2007; Back et al., 2020). Transparency regulations, such as timely reporting of block trades, diminish this advantage by making valuable information available to the market. However, increased transparency could expose dealers to adverse price movements from front-running strategies, harming their profits, and potentially reduce their incentives to intermediate blocks or offer competitive quotes to initiators.The experimental evidence from Bloomfield and O'Hara (2000) suggests that dealers prefer not to disclose their trades and that non-disclosing dealers earn substantially higher profits. Empirical studies generally find that trade reporting improves customer outcomes and reduces dealer spreads in corporate bonds (Bessembinder et al., 2006; Edwards et al., 2007; Goldstein et al., 2007; O'Hara et al., 2018), municipal bonds (Schultz, 2012) and agency mortgage-backed securities (Schultz and Song, 2019). However, Gemmill (1996) finds no significant change in bid-ask spreads across trade reporting regimes for stocks on the London Stock Exchange. Chalmers et al. (2021) report lower customer trading costs with reduced trade reporting delay for municipal bonds; however, the study does not examine the block market nor the outcomes for specific customer-types within the intermediation process.We exploit variations in trade reporting regulation to empirically validate the theoretical prediction (e.g., Naik et al., 1999; Back et al., 2020) that transparent regimes level the information playing field. Our findings offer evidence of the mechanism envisioned by theorists — timely trade disclosure allows receivers to observe the occurrence and terms of block trades, helping them manage adverse selection risk in the block trade process.7 We also identify customer types that benefit more from increased transparency, adding nuance to existing research.
article.3. Data, methodology and descriptive sta: 3.1. Data sources and sample constructionTo construct our initial sample of corporate bonds, we use the Mergent Fixed Income Securities Database (FISD). We select non-puttable or convertible U.S. Corporate Debentures and U.S. Corporate Bank Notes (identified as bond type CDEB or USBN) that have complete issuance information, including offering date, amount, and maturity. This selection results in an initial sample of 55,842 bonds.8For corporate bond transactions, we use the enhanced Trade Reporting and Compliance Engine (TRACE) data provided by FINRA, which includes detailed information such as dealer identification numbers, unmasked trade size, and comprehensive trade data, encompassing trades disseminated to the public as well as trades not so disseminated, spanning the period from July 2002 to November 2021. We link the FISD data to TRACE data using the CUSIP identifier, resulting in a sample of 39,801 bonds and 147.9 million trades.Table 1 outlines the impact of additional data filters we apply. We exclude bonds with fewer than five trades over the nearly twenty-year period. Trades are excluded if their reported size exceeds the bond's offering amount, if they are reported after the bond's outstanding amount is reported as zero, or if they have execution dates before July 2002. We also exclude primary market transactions as well as secondary market transactions occurring immediately after issuance.9 Additionally, following the literature, we exclude trades by a specific large dealer that, during 2014, reported an immediately offsetting transaction for most of its principal trades with an affiliated offshore entity. After implementing these filters, our final sample consists of 138.5 million secondary market transactions in 38,762 distinct CUSIPs.Table 1. Measuring price effects of block trades in corporate bondsIn the corporate bond market, where quotation data are often unavailable, researchers have adapted liquidity measures from equity markets, relying solely on transactions price data. One widely used metric for estimating trading costs is the dealer round-trip cost (Goldstein et al., 2007), which measures the difference between the average prices at which dealers sell and buy bonds, serving as a substitute for the bid-ask spread used in equity markets. In this study, we also calculate dealer round-trip costs to quantify dealer spreads in block trade intermediation. Further, following the block trade literature (Kraus and Stoll, 1972), we estimate the price effects of corporate bond block trades.Fig. 1 illustrates the price dynamics when a dealer sells a block of bonds, denoted as size Q, at the execution time tblock to an initiating customer (Dealer Block Sell). The negotiated block price (PB) is often set away from the bond's equilibrium value leading up to the trade, typically higher for a Dealer Block Sell and lower for a Dealer Block Buy. This mark-up or mark-down compensates the dealer for absorbing the block into inventory, reflects the increased risk of adverse selection inherent in large trades, and reflects a service fee for locating counterparties with latent trading interests. In the figure, a Dealer Block Sell occurs at a price PB that is higher than the pre-trade equilibrium value PPRE, and the markup (PB - PPRE) represents the trading cost borne by the initiator. In our main analysis, this hypothetical pre-trade equilibrium value is calculated using the volume-weighted average trade prices (VWAP) across all dealers in the week preceding the block trade. We consider several alternate definitions in the robustness analysis. In Eq. (1), the variable D equals +1 for a Dealer Block Sell and equals −1 for a Dealer Block Buy.(1)Blockinitiatorcost,I(Q)=D*[Ln(PB)−Ln(PPRE)]*100The permanent price impact, P(Q), represents the change in the market's perception of the bond's equilibrium value following the block trade. In the figure, the post-trade equilibrium value PPOST is higher than the pre-trade equilibrium value (PPRE), reflecting the information effects of a block trade initiated by a buyer. We calculate the post-trade equilibrium value using the bond's VWAP across all dealers in the week after the block end date (the block end date is defined in Section 3.5). The temporary price impact, T(Q), captures the short-term liquidity effects, measured by the price deviation caused by the block trade's price pressure, and its reversal to the new equilibrium value following the trade.10(2)Temporarypriceimpact,T(Q)=D*[Ln(PB)−Ln(PPOST)]*100(3)Permanentpriceimpact,P(Q)=D*[Ln(PPOST)−Ln(PPRE)]*100Unlike the US equities TAQ database, which lacks market maker identifiers, the enhanced TRACE corporate bond data includes detailed buy and sell trade identifier and dealer codes. The ability to sign trades is crucial for the block market analysis, as the expected price effects of Dealer Block Buys and Sells are opposite, and combining these categories would obscure the price effects of interest. Dealer codes at the trade-level allow us to comprehensively track the buying and selling activities of individual dealers. We leverage these data attributes to link the block trade to the dealer's related trades with receivers, detailed in Section 3.5.Having identified the related receiver trades, we adapt the equity block market methodology to the corporate bond market by decomposing the temporary price impact into dealer and receiver spreads. In a Dealer Block Sell, the dealer gains by repurchasing the bonds from receivers at a lower price (POFF) to cover the block position, thus earning a dealer spread (PB - POFF). The dealer's primary risk involves finding receivers willing to sell at a sufficiently low price and the potential delay in offsetting the position. We calculate dealer spreads by comparing the block trade price PB with the VWAP of the receiver trades (POFF), which represents the price at which the dealer offsets the block position.(4)Dealerspread,D(Q)=D*[Ln(PB)−Ln(POFF)]*100In scenarios where dealers face significant inventory risk – such as with illiquid bonds, block trades, or during market stress – receivers can capture a portion of the temporary price effects by providing liquidity to the dealer. We measure receiver spreads as the difference between the price at which the receivers offset the block position and the equilibrium value after the block trade (e.g., POFF - PPOST for a Dealer Block Sell). In Fig. 1, a positive value means receivers sold the bond to the dealer at a higher price than its value if continuing to hold the bond. Receivers bear the risk that this spread could turn negative, particularly if the block has significant information effects. In such cases (PPOST > POFF for a Block Dealer Sell), a negative spread indicates that receivers were worse off selling the bond to the dealer compared to its post-trade equilibrium value.(5)Receiverspread,R(Q)=D*[Ln(POFF)−Ln(PPOST)]*100Fig. 1, Panel A, illustrates a Dealer Block Sell where the initiator incurs a 20 bp cost, calculated as the difference between the block price and the pre-trade equilibrium value (PB - PPRE). Panel B further decomposes the temporary price impact of 15 bp into a dealer spread of 10 bp and a receiver spread of 5 bp. The dealer spread (PB - POFF) represents the dealer's realized gain or loss, while the receiver spread (POFF - PPOST) is a notional outcome, as the receiver's actual holding period may not align with the timeframe used to measure the bond's equilibrium value after the trade.Fig. 1, Panel C illustrates how the delay events that shorten the trade reporting timeframe improves receiver outcomes, particularly when information effects are significant (permanent price impact, PPOST - PPRE, is 7.5 bp). Before the reporting of the block trade, the receiver sells the bonds at price POFF-BEF which is closer to the pre-trade equilibrium value (PPRE), leading to receiver spread of −5 bp (PPOST - POFF-BEF). TRACE reporting rules do not disclose the initiator's identity and actual block size to the public, known only to the dealer, limiting receiver's ability to fully assess information effects.11 Once the block trade is reported, the market adjusts the bond's equilibrium value, taking into account new information, such as the occurrence of the block trade and its reported price (PB). For example, observing a higher initiator cost might signal the presence of an informed block buyer or a mega-block, leading the market to revise the valuation higher from PPRE to PPOST. Following the report of the block trade, the receiver sells at a higher price of POFF-AFT earning a positive spread of 2.5 bp (PPOST - POFF-AFT). Timely reporting thus helps receivers negotiate a better trade price with the dealer, enhancing their outcomes.3.3. Measuring savings to receiversTheoretical model, such as Grossman (1992) and Madhavan (1995) describe receivers as natural counterparties to block initiators, with trading interests that are known to the dealer. According to the model of Burdett and O'Hara (1987), although receivers may infer the block's presence, they may not be able to fully assess its information effects. Nevertheless, this model suggest that reduced trading costs are a significant benefit to receivers, who would incur larger costs to initiate a similar trade. Risk-averse receivers may also prefer to avoid execution delays and the higher costs associated with smaller trades in OTC markets; by participating as a counterparty, receivers acquire larger positions at known prices and avoid the premiums for initiating trades. Moreover, receivers may strengthen their relationships with the intermediating dealer, obtaining transaction cost benefits for future trades.To test these predictions, we estimate cost savings for receivers using two methods that account for trade size, market conditions and market side (buying or selling). The first method computes the costs receivers would incur if they initiated a hypothetical trade of similar size based on a block trading cost model; these model-imputed costs are then compared to actual receiver spreads.The second method calculates the hypothetical price for building or liquidating a position using all TRACE transaction prices close in time to the block trade, specific to the relevant market side. Comparing this hypothetical price with the receiver's offsetting trade price (POFF) provides a second estimate of cost savings. These savings could potentially reflect the benefits of trading with relationship dealers in the block market compared to executing multiple, smaller transactions with non-relationship counterparts. These measures are described in detail in Section 4.3.3.4. Block market activity through timeFig. 2 shows the market share of block trades within the corporate bond market from 2002 to 2021. We define a block trade as a dealer's sale or purchase with a customer in a single transaction with par value of at least $15 million and report the market share for block size thresholds of $15, $20 and $30 million. During this period, the corporate bond market experienced significant events, including the phased implementation of trade reporting (2002–2005), the global financial crisis (2007–2009), post-crisis bank regulations (2011–2014), and the rise of electronic trading platforms. Despite these market developments, the market share of block trades remained remarkably stable, averaging about 13 % of the trading volume reported on TRACE (Fig. 2, Panel A). Block trades constitute a higher share of trading volume in investment grade bonds compared to high yield bonds (Fig. 2, Panel B).Download: Download high-res image (745KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Block Trading Activity 2002–2021.The figures show block trading statistics over the July 2002 to November 2021 sample period. Figure A shows block trading volume as a proportion of total volume. A block trade is a dealer's sale or purchase with a customer in a single transaction with a par value of at least $15 million (blue solid), $20 million (long red dash), and $30 million (short green dash). Figure B shows block trading volume for blocks of at least $15 million as a share of total volume, distinguished between investment-grade bonds (blue solid line) and high-yield bonds (long red dash). Figure C shows the percentage of block trades that are prearranged, defined as those offset in a single trade within 15 min.We also examine prearranged blocks trades, commonly referred to as “riskless principal” trades, where the dealer immediately offsets the block position by executing a single opposite-direction trade within 15 min, effectively acting as a broker or agent (see Harris, 2016; Choi et al., 2024). Fig. 2, Panel C shows that these prearranged block trades consistently represent about 11 % of the total block volume throughout the sample period.12Appendix Fig. 1 shows that the patterns in block market trading activity remain consistent even after accounting for changes in bond attributes and market conditions through regression analysis over the sample period. Our findings do not support industry concerns that the relevance of the block market for corporate bonds has declined over time.3.5. Identifying block trades and receiver trades in TRACE dataWe define “triggering” or “initiating” block trades as those with a par value of at least $15 million, and we consider four block size definitions throughout the majority of our analyses: 1) trades greater than $15 million, 2) trades between $15 million and $20 million, 3) trades between $20 million and $30 million, and 4) trades greater than $30 million.For each block trade, we identify the dealers’ offsetting trades with receivers as follows. We begin by retaining all trades executed by the block dealer in the same bond during the week following the block trade date, covering five trading days. As an example, if the block trade occurs on a Wednesday, the “block week” extends from that Wednesday to next Tuesday. Then, starting with the block volume, we cumulate the dealer's (signed) trading volume in the bond. If the cumulative imbalance reaches or crosses zero during the block week, the block is classified as “fully offset”. The “block end” time is defined as the earlier of either when the block is fully offset or the end of the block week. Receiver trades are identified as those that offset the dealer's block position before the block end time. For example, in a Dealer Block Sell scenario, receiver trades are those where the dealer acts as a buyer in the transaction.We focus on the block week spanning five trading days for two reasons. First, empirical evidence on dealer behavior supports this timeframe. Bessembinder et al. (2018) show that the proportion of weekly trading volume held in dealers’ weekend inventory is typically <10 %. Goldstein and Hotchkiss (2020) report a median and mean dealer holding period of one day and ten days, respectively. Hollifield et al. (2020) show that over 70 % of large trades are matched within a week. Second, as the time between the block trade and receiver trades increases, the link between the triggering block trade and the dealers’ related trades of opposite sign becomes less clear.We categorize the earliest large trade as the initiating block trade; thus, any subsequent block trades in the same bond by the same dealer, occurring before the block end time and with the opposite sign, are classified as receiver trades rather than initiating trades. Receiver trades can include non-block trades executed on the block day prior to the block execution time, as well as any offsetting trades, regardless of size, that occur after the block execution time. After the block end time, any subsequent trades by the dealer in the bond that meets the block definition is classified as a new initiating block trade. Appendix A provides detailed examples and a thorough explanation of the methodology.We acknowledge the difficulty in identifying the initiating block trade. When dealers prearrange block trades, it often becomes ambiguous whether the initiating customer was a buyer or a seller, as the dealer intermediates both a block buy and sell of similar size within a short time frame. To address this, our sample selection criteria require that the block trade meet the $15 million size threshold and are not reported as an “agency” trade on TRACE, nor flagged as a prearranged trade by our matching algorithm.13 Section 4.3 discusses potential classification errors and the robustness of our main findings in detail.To ensure consistency across our price effect measures, we focus on block trades where we have complete data for initiator cost, dealer spread and receiver spread. This requires the bond to have at least one trade in the week before the block trade, one in the week following the block trade, and at least one offsetting trade by the block dealer during the block week. We also exclude blocks with extreme price effect values: initiator cost, dealer or receiver spread, or permanent price impact exceeding $50 (likely indicative of errors), as well as blocks with a block trade price below $5.14 Trading costs are winsorized at the 1 % and 99 % levels.3.6. Adverse selection risk and receiving investor outcomesThe block market is characterized by heightened asymmetric information, with large trades often motivated by private information. The theoretical literature suggests that receivers are typically the least informed participant in this market. Although they may infer the presence of a block trade when approached by a dealer, they do not observe the initiator's identity, the signals exchanged during the negotiations between the dealer and initiator, or the true size of the block trade. This limits their ability to anticipate the trade's information effects. Consequently, receiver spreads are expected to decrease and may even turn negative when the block trade is informed.In this section, we test these theoretical predictions concerning how asymmetric information affects receivers. We begin by examining receiver outcomes based on the observed permanent price impact of block trades, a well-accepted measure of adverse selection risk for liquidity providers (see Kraus and Stoll, 1972). As illustrated in Fig. 1, it follows mechanically that a higher ex-post permanent price impact leads to a greater change in the bond's equilibrium value (PPOST - PPRE). However, the impact on the receiver depends on their ability to anticipate the block's information effects and to effectively negotiate the price (POFF) at which they are willing to offset the dealer's position.We sort our block trade sample into deciles based on permanent price impact. Fig. 3 Panels A-C illustrates average receiver spreads, dealer spreads, and initiator costs across these deciles. Panel A shows a clear negative relationship between permanent price impact and receiver spreads: in Decile 1 (lowest impact), receivers earn large positive spreads; by Decile 5, spreads are near zero; and in Decile 10, receivers earn large negative spreads, indicating they fare worse when the trade is informed. In contrast, Panel B shows that dealers effectively manage adverse selection risk: in Decile 1, dealers earn large positive spreads, but spreads remain stable at about 10 bp from Decile 5 through Decile 10. Panel C shows a positive relationship between permanent price impact and initiator cost, suggesting that dealers anticipate informed blocks and typically negotiate block prices that reflect the trade's information content. Thus, market participants may infer dealers’ expectations about a block's information content by observing the block trade price. These results highlight how timely disclosure of the block trade can improve price discovery, which we consider in detail in Section 5.Download: Download high-res image (406KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. These studies find that sustained buying is typically associated with information effects, while sustained selling does not exhibit the same trend. This evidence aligns with research in the equity block market, where blocks initiated by a buyer exhibit a more significant and positive permanent price impact than blocks initiated by a seller (Barclay and Warner, 1993; Keim and Madhavan, 1996).The literature often attributes asymmetric information effects to the differing motivations behind large trades (Chan and Lakonishok, 1995). Blocks initiated by a buyer are usually interpreted as signals of insider information or investor optimism about future positive news, leading to a stronger market reaction. On the other hand, blocks initiated by sellers are often perceived to be driven by liquidity needs or portfolio rebalancing rather than by adverse insider information, resulting in a less pronounced market reaction. Additionally, prior research ascribes asymmetric temporary price effects to the inventory considerations of block dealers. According to Kraus and Stoll (1972), dealers are more likely to use their own capital to facilitate a Dealer Block Buy, while they may rely on existing inventory to facilitate a Dealer Block Sell due to constraints on short selling. This reliance on inventory can lead to smaller temporary price effects for Dealer Block Sells compared to Dealer Block Buys.Table 4, Panel A reports cost measures for block trades based on their direction, similar to those reported in Table 3. Trade classification issues and sub-sample analysesOur methodology relies on an accurate classification of customer type – the initiating block trade and the related offsetting trades by the block dealer. The economics underlying receiving investor participationReceivers often bear adverse selection costs and earn negative spreads, indicating they are not adequately compensated for the liquidity services they provide to dealers. This raises the question of why receivers continue to participate in the block market. Burdett and O'Hara (1987) suggest that a key incentive is the ability to reduce trading costs, particularly when receivers need to establish or liquidate a large position. To test this hypothesis, we estimate the potential costs savings for receivers using the two methods outlined in Section 3.3. These methods evaluate whether receivers would have faced worse outcomes had they initiated a trade themselves, rather than serving as a counterparty to the block trade.The first method relies on a trading cost model reported in Appendix Table 5.22 For each block trade, we calculate an “imputed initiator cost” based on the model's fitted value, using the weighted average size of receiver trades linked to the block trade.23 We then compare the actual receiver spread with this imputed trading cost of a hypothetical trade of comparable size. This indicates that receivers are better off participating in offsetting trades with the dealer than initiating a block buy of similar size. For Dealer Block Sells, the cost savings are slightly positive at 1 bp, reflecting an average receiver spread of −12 bp and imputed costs of 13 bp. This suggests that the higher adverse selection costs for receivers when they participate in Dealer Block Sells are fully offset by the liquidity premium of initiating a block sale of comparable size. These findings align with Burdett and O'Hara's (1987) prediction that, while receivers may incur losses on informed blocks, they still achieve cost savings by acting as counterparties to the block trades. This highlights the economic rationale underlying why receivers continue to participate in the block market.However, our results reveal a potential fragility: if information asymmetry becomes too severe, it could deter receiver participation. Fig. 3, Panel D shows receivers’ costs savings by permanent price impact decile, with the blue line indicating that cost savings for deciles 8 to 10 (highest impact) are negative.24We recognize that our trading cost model is calibrated using block trades of at least $15 million, whereas receiver trades typically involve smaller sizes, often below $15 million. Prior research in corporate bonds show that trading costs decrease with larger trade sizes (Edwards et al., 2007; Bessembinder et al., 2018). Therefore, the imputed initiator costs derived from larger block trades likely underestimate the actual execution costs for smaller receiver trades. We do not extend our trading cost model to include smaller trades because accurate classification of initiator versus receiver trades becomes more challenging as trade sizes decrease.In the second method, we calculate a hypothetical price (PHYP) for each block trade, reflecting the price that receivers would likely achieve to build or liquidate a typical position using TRACE transactions data. This price is derived from VWAP of TRACE-reported customer trades across all dealers on the same market side as the receiver trades and then compared to the offsetting price (POFF) of the receiver trades associated with the block trade. For Dealer Block Buys, the savings are calculated as the difference between the hypothetical price and the receiver offset price (POFF). Conversely, for Dealer Block Sells, savings are determined by the difference between the receiver offset price (POFF) and the hypothetical price. Because the hypothetical price is calculated using all trades, while receiver trades are generally larger round lot trades of at least $1 million, this method could potentially overestimate the benefits receivers achieve through the block market process, as larger trades might secure better terms than smaller TRACE trades.Table 6, Panel B reports the cost savings based on hypothetical prices calculated using all trades in the week following the block week. The savings range from 10 bp to 22 bp for Dealer Block Buys and from 0 bp to 11 bp for Dealer Block sells. The results suggest that receivers generally fare better in Dealer Block Buys, and at the very least, no worse in Dealer Block Sells – even when information effects are present – compared to building or liquidating a similar position outside of the block market process.In Table 6, Panel C, Column (4), we introduce a significant modification: the hypothetical price is now calculated based on the VWAP across all dealers during the week before the block trade. This analysis offers a valuable perspective on receiver savings, reflecting the trade terms available to receivers before the block trade's information affects prices. For both Block Dealer Buys and Sells, the potential savings in Column (4) are positive and of similar magnitude at 11–12 bp. The symmetry of savings reported in Panel C, Column (4), combined with the asymmetry of savings reported in Panel B, underscores that receivers are unable to anticipate information effects, leading to asymmetrical savings when they participate after the trade report. Notably, when permanent price impact is substantial, receivers are relatively better off liquidating a position before the block trade is reported.

Block trade disclosure and receiver o: The results suggest that while receivers benefit from trading cost savings in the block market, these benefits diminish when information asymmetry is high. Market transparency, a key factor influencing the trading environment, affects the extent to which dealers enjoy information advantages in bilateral negotiations with receivers. During our sample period, the corporate bond market experienced various trade reporting rule changes, generally leading to greater transparency. However, these changes have been controversial, with industry groups often opposing the reporting of block trades. In this section, we examine the impact of trade disclosure regulation on the block market.5.1. Transparency events that introduce post-trade reportingOur analysis begins with transparency events, specifically the introduction of mandatory reporting of corporate bond trades to FINRA's TRACE system. Transparency was introduced in stages, beginning with trade report initiation for a small group of actively traded bonds in July 2002. This analysis focuses on public bonds with staggered trade reporting in 2003, 2004, and 2005, as well as non-public 144A bonds in 2014. The dissemination dates for these bonds are as follows: March 3, 2003, October 1, 2004, February 7, 2005, and June 27, 2014, respectively.For bonds with TRACE initiation in March 2003, the period before trade report initiation is restricted to 8 months, given the TRACE system was implemented in July 2002. Therefore, we select an 8-month period after transparency and maintain a consistent 16-month window for all four samples. Specifically, for each sample, we study a 16-month period before and after the initiation of trade reporting.25 To be included in our sample, a bond must have at least one block trade in both the period before and the period after transparency. We retain block trades that are not marked as agency trades, are not identified as prearranged blocks by our algorithm, have a block trade price of at least $5.00, and have non-missing trading cost measures. These filters yield 622 block trades in 132 issues for the 2003 sample, 863 block trades in 192 issues for the 2004 sample, 47 block trades in 9 issues for the 2005 sample, and 912 block trades in 183 issues for the 2014 sample.Table 7 reports the results of the impact of transparency events on initiator costs, dealer spreads, and receiver spreads for our combined sample of block trades. Panel A reports univariate analyses of trading costs before and after TRACE initiation, while Panel B reports similar analyses in a multivariate setting. Post-Transparency is an indicator variable that equals one for block trades conducted after the initiation of trade reporting and zero otherwise. We implement issue-level fixed effects in the regressions, allowing us to estimate the within-bond impact of trade reporting. We also include trade-level controls such as the natural log of trade size and whether the intermediary is a small dealer, an indicator for on-the-run bonds, and market controls including the trailing weekly corporate bond market index return, the trailing weekly S&P index return, the change in the average three-month LIBOR interest rate, and the change in the VIX index over the previous week. To account for dealer inventory risk, we report results with standard errors clustered at the issue level, given the risk is influenced by each issue's distinct trading activity and anticipated demand from receivers, which can vary across issues from the same issuer.26 We also report results with standard errors clustered at the issuer level to capture information-driven trading tied to firm-level factors. The univariate results in Table 7 Panel A indicate that average dealer spreads for facilitating blocks decrease from 23 bp to 18 bp after trade report initiation, while the regression results in Panel B, columns (3) and (4) show an 8 bp reduction in dealer spreads. In both panels, this decline is statistically significant and economically large, as an 8 bp reduction represents one-third of the pre-transparency dealer spreads of 23 bp. Average initiator costs in Panel A, column (1) do not differ meaningfully before and after trade reporting, averaging about 19 bp. Similarly, in Panel B, columns (1) and (2), the coefficient on post-Transparency is not statistically significant. Therefore, increased transparency, while reducing dealer spreads, has no significant impact on trade execution costs for block initiators.Receivers appear to be the primary beneficiaries of trade report initiation in the block market. As shown in Panel A, column (5), average receiver spreads increase from −10 bp before trade reporting to −2 bps after, reflecting a marked improvement. In the multivariate analysis, the regression coefficient of 11 bp in Panel B indicates an economically significant improvement in receiver outcomes relative to the pre-transparency spread of −10 bp. This improvement is statistically significant at the 10 % level in column (5), but not statistically significant in column (6). However, following trade report initiation, some receiver trades may not fully capture the benefits of transparency due to permissible delays in block trade reporting – a topic we will explore further in the next section.Prior studies on the corporate bond market generally conclude that trade report initiation reduces dealer spreads and improves execution costs for customers. Our study contributes further nuance to this evidence by examining the block market, where we find that the effects of trade report initiation vary by customer-type, reflecting their information disadvantages during bilateral negotiations with dealers. In line with theoretical models (e.g., Madhavan, 1995), we show that mandatory trade reporting primarily benefits less-informed customers (receivers), while the outcomes for more informed customers (initiators) remain largely unaffected.We further investigate whether dealers reduce their facilitation of block trades in response to narrower spreads after trade report initiation in Appendix Table 6. Both univariate and multivariate analyses indicate that block volume relative to total volume remain stable, suggesting no decline in dealer participation with trade report initiation.27 However, with mandated trade reporting, dealers offset a larger portion of block trades within the block week and are more likely to fully offset their positions. This shift indicates that while dealers continue to intermediate blocks, they exhibit heightened sensitivity to the increased inventory risk associated with holding these positions.5.2. Delay events that shorten the trade reporting timeframeAs per regulatory rules, FINRA-registered dealers are required to report all completed corporate bond trades (customers do not report trades) to FINRA's TRACE system. The TRACE system disseminates trade information, including price and capped size, immediately upon receipt of a trade report from a dealer. Notably, FINRA stipulates a maximum permissible delay between the execution of the trade and its reporting to TRACE. In the block market, this delay enables dealers to leverage information observed during their bilateral negotiations with initiator, allowing them to potentially offset a portion of the block at a favorable price before the block trade is publicly reported.In this section, we study delay events, focusing on the impact of the rules governing the trade reporting timeframe to the TRACE system. During our sample period, FINRA shortened the reporting delay in three stages, from 75 min to 45 min, then to 30 min, and finally to the current 15 min. We examine block trades during these four reporting regimes: July 2002-September 2003 (75-minute reporting); October 2003-September 2004 (45-minute reporting); October 2004-June 2005 (30-minute reporting); and July 2005-June 2006 (15-minute reporting).For this analysis, we start with our original sample of 205,104 block trades. We then exclude block trades for bonds that were not yet eligible for TRACE-dissemination in each regime, as well as those reported >24 h after the execution timestamp (which are likely errors). We retain block trades with at least one receiver trade both before and after the block trade report timestamp.28 For each reporting regime, we separately calculate the cost measures for receiver trades with execution timestamps occurring before and after the block trade report timestamp. Dealer and receiver spreads are calculated using trade-size weighted average prices, resulting in two observations for each block trade. Using this approach, we implement block-level fixed effects in the regressions, allowing us to estimate the within-block impact on receivers upon observing the block trade, while controlling for trade-specific, bond-specific, and market wide factors on the spread measures.29The results are reported in Table 8, Panel A. We find that receivers obtain better terms on offsetting trades that occur after the report of the block trade compared to those before the report. Specifically, in the July 2002-September 2003 regime, receivers lose 16 bp on offsetting trades that occur prior to block trade report but lose only 7 bp on offsetting trades that occur after block trade report, representing an improvement in trade terms of about 50 %. These improvements are consistent across all reporting regimes, with receiver spread improvements ranging from 5 bp to 11 bp. Dealer spreads exhibit a similar pattern, but with the opposite sign. Do delay events change dealer behavior?We next examine how dealers change their trading behavior when the trade report timeframe is shortened across the four reporting regimes in Table 9. article.6. Conclusions and implications: This study highlights the important role of receiving investors in the block market. We develop a methodology to identify a comprehensive sample of 205,104 corporate bond block trades and 690,418 related receiver trades from 2002 to 2021. Using this sample, we test theoretical predictions about the impact of the information environment on receivers – a topic that has received limited attention in the literature. Consistent with Burdett and O'Hara (1987), our results suggest that receivers benefit by efficiently establishing or liquidating positions at lower costs through offsetting trades with block dealers. This provides a clear economic rationale for receiver participation in the block market. However, we also find that receivers primarily bear adverse selection costs, whereas dealers, who have access to information about the block initiator's identity and trade terms, can better anticipate informed trading. This outcome aligns with the market's institutional structure, where receivers remain the least informed party in the block trade process.We also examine the effects of regulatory changes over our two-decade sample period, focusing on the impact of block trade disclosure on market participants. Our analysis of the transparency events that introduce post-trade reporting for corporate bonds suggests that transparency's effects vary by customer type. Less-informed receivers benefit from transparency, while outcomes for better-informed initiators appear largely unchanged. Additionally, we examine delay events pertaining to FINRA's phased reduction of the maximum permissible delay between trade execution and reporting. Our findings empirically support the theoretical mechanisms suggesting that timely trade reporting improves customer outcomes by partially revealing the dealer's private information, thereby improving customers’ ability to negotiate better trade terms.Despite the broader movement towards increased transparency in fixed income markets, regulators have recently considered proposals to delay the reporting of block trades in corporate bonds and swaps. In June 2019, FINRA proposed a pilot program to extend the reporting delay for corporate bond block trades from 15 min to 48 h, following a recommendation from SEC's Fixed Income Market Structure Advisory Committee (FIMSAC). Much of the regulatory discussions have focused on the potential benefits of delayed trade reporting for block initiators and dealers.34 Our study provides evidence on the economic motivations behind dealer support for this proposal, showing that longer reporting delays provide dealers with more time to offset positions, thereby improving their spreads. However, these delays adversely affect receivers, to whom the dealer distributes the block.We also offer guidance on a recently adopted SEC rule that shortens the trade reporting timeframe from the current 15-minutes to one minute after trade execution in various fixed-income markets. Our results suggest that shorter delay will provide receivers with timely, value-relevant information during negotiations with dealers. However, we also observe that dealers, across all reporting regimes, consistently offset about 20 % of the block position before the block trade is reported. As the new rule is implemented, regulators should carefully evaluate whether a one-minute delay allows dealers–especially smaller ones–sufficient time to manage block positions effectively.Our analysis shows that block market activity has remained stable throughout our sample period, suggesting that greater transparency has not led dealers to withdraw from the block market, even as it enhances market fairness for other participants. Nonetheless, regulators should monitor whether shorter reporting delays could eventually reduce block market activity, potentially disadvantaging large buy-side institutions. As Bessembinder et al. (2020) note, the optimal transparency regime may vary across fixed income markets. Given the differences in clientele and trading frequency among asset classes affected by the SEC's newly adopted rule – such as corporate bonds, agency debt, asset backed securities and agency pass-through mortgage-backed securities – cross-asset analysis will be essential to understand the rule's impact. Finally, our findings on the benefits of timely trade disclosure are relevant for regulatory efforts aimed at increasing transparency in European fixed income markets and in aggregate for U.S. Treasury securities and OTC U.S. equity markets.
